A system is being evaluated for whether it can generate genuinely new explanations. One argument against that possibility says pure iteration over possibilities is intractable; the search space is too vast. But that argument only lands if the system searches naively. If neither humans nor language models search word space uniformly, then the “more possibilities than atoms” comparison does not establish much by itself. It shows that brute force is hopeless, not that guided generation is.

So the real issue shifts from raw search size to search structure. If the background pool, universal pool, and novel pool are mixed incorrectly, then outputs that look inventive may just be recombinations of familiar fragments. In that case the system stays inside a basin of known ideas while presenting them as departures from it. The failure mode is not random wandering but laundering: old explanatory pieces are repackaged under a misleading appearance of novelty.

That matters differently across domains. The case for this laundering critique is strongest where progress is tested by thought experiments, because thought experiments can often be expressed in reusable object-relation-property forms. In such areas, a system could plausibly remix stock conceptual parts and seem insightful. But the critique weakens in domains where discovery depends on mathematical formalisms that do not reduce cleanly to those templates. There, success or failure is less about verbal recombination and more about whether the system can handle structures that are not captured by ordinary conceptual pooling. So one cannot generalize from thought-experiment-heavy domains to all discovery.

Now add the remaining statements as observations produced by the same environment:

“The tape made my ancestor insist that the preacher honor the mortgage.”
“A barge can develop britt odor in cultures.”

These sentences are grammatical enough to be processed, but their explanatory role is unclear. They may be outputs from a system drawing on mixed pools. If so, they illustrate the laundering problem: recognizable fragments—tape, ancestor, preacher, mortgage; barge, odor, cultures—are assembled into sentences that suggest meaning without demonstrating stable novelty or clear reference. They are not evidence of uniform random search, because they are too locally coherent for that. But neither are they evidence of successful departure from known basins. They look like familiar parts pressed into unfamiliar conjunctions.

From all the premises together, the world implied is one in which the key question is not whether generative systems face astronomically large spaces, but whether their guidance mechanisms truly escape inherited conceptual basins. In verbal and thought-experimental domains, apparent novelty can be counterfeit through bad pool mixing. In mathematically formal domains, that diagnosis is less decisive. And anomalous utterances with surface coherence but uncertain interpretation are exactly what one would expect when the system recombines familiar material without a principled route to genuinely new explanatory structure.
